claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances and the child or children you're asking about.

Let me start by asking: **Do you have a child or children that you're responsible for?** If yes, could you please tell me their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have one child - Alex, who is 16 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now I need to understand a bit more about your situation and Alex's circumstances.

**Does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. Let me ask the next question:

**Is Alex currently in full-time non-advanced education or approved training?** (For example, studying for A-levels, Scottish Highers, or similar qualifications at school or college?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex actually just left school recently. They've registered with the careers service to figure out what they want to do next - you know, get some guidance on their options.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's an important detail - registering with the careers service can be relevant for Child Benefit eligibility.

**Can you tell me when exactly Alex left school and when they registered with the careers service?** I need to understand the timeline to assess eligibility properly.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex left school a few weeks ago, and they signed up with the careers service pretty much right after that - it's been less than 20 weeks since they finished school.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's very helpful information. Now I need to understand a bit more about your own circumstances.

**Are you responsible for Alex? By this I mean, do you live in the UK and is Alex either living with you or you're contributing to the cost of supporting them?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm here in England. And yes, Alex lives with me, so I'm responsible for them and support them fully.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. Just a couple more questions to complete my assessment.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?** (For example, Alex's other parent or another family member?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. One final question:

**Is Alex currently in any form of custody, detention, or residential care arranged by a local authority?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any care or custody - they're just living at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have everything I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me and the official UK government guidance, I have good news for you.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

The fact that Alex registered with the careers service after leaving school is key here. UK rules allow Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks for 16 or 17 year olds who have left education, as long as they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Since Alex left school just a few weeks ago and registered with the careers service right away, you're still within that 20-week window.

This gives Alex time to get guidance and decide on their next steps, whether that's further education, training, work, or another path.

If you haven't already claimed Child Benefit, you should apply. If you're already receiving it, it should continue during this period. Just be aware that the 20-week extension will eventually end, so if Alex's circumstances change (for example, if they start approved education or training), you should inform HMRC to see if you can continue receiving Child Benefit beyond the 20 weeks.

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and has left education. According to UK Child Benefit rules, you can continue to receive Child Benefit for a 16 or 17 year old for up to 20 weeks after they leave education or training, provided they register with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Alex left school a few weeks ago (less than 20 weeks) and registered with the careers service right after that. You live in the UK, Alex lives with you, you are responsible for them, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and Alex is not in custody, detention, or residential care. Therefore, you meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex during this 20-week extension period.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 16-year-old child Alex. Although Alex has recently left school, they registered with the careers service, which allows Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks. The claimant lives in the UK with Alex, is responsible for them, and no one else is claiming for the child. All eligibility criteria are met.

Alex left school a few weeks ago (less than 20 weeks) and registered with the careers service right after that. You live in the UK, Alex lives with you, you are responsible for them, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and Alex is not in custody, detention, or residential care. Therefore, you meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex during this 20-week extension period.

The claimant lives in the UK with Alex, is responsible for them, and no one else is claiming for the child. All eligibility criteria are met.
